title: NOC 84101 – Oil and gas well drilling and related workers and services operators | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 84101 represents the specialized intermediate workforce that ensures the mechanical and chemical integrity of Canada’s oil and gas wells. As a TEER 4 occupation, these workers serve as the critical link between entry-level laborers and senior drillers. From operating high-pressure hydraulic pumping systems for well stimulation to maintaining the massive diesel motors that power the rigs, these professionals are indispensable to the energy supply chain. In 2026, as the industry transitions toward high-efficiency and lower-emission extraction, the demand for technically proficient operators is at an all-time high.

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category8 – Natural resources, agriculture and related production occupationsTEER Level4Major Group84 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionSub-Major Group841 – Workers in natural resources, agriculture and related productionMinor Group8410 – Mine service workers and operators in oil and gas drillingNOC Code84101

duties: Drilling Support: Align and manipulate sections of pipe or drill stem from the derrick platform; operate and maintain drilling mud systems and pumps.
Mechanical Maintenance: Operate and maintain drilling rig diesel motors, transmissions, and other mechanical equipment (Motorhand duties).
Well Stimulation: Drive specialized service trucks to well sites; assemble pumping equipment and operate hydraulic systems to pump chemicals, sand, or cement into the well.
Monitoring: Read gauges to monitor pressure, density, and flow rates, adjusting pumping procedures in real-time.
Safety & Supervision: Assist in rig setup/teardown and supervise floorhands and laborers to ensure a safe work environment.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is mandatory.
Work Experience: Usually requires at least one year of experience as a floorhand or laborer in the oil patch.
Training: Up to three months of on-the-job training with an experienced operator is standard for well services.
Certifications: * H2S Alive (Hydrogen Sulphide Awareness).First Aid and CPR (Level C).Fall Protection (specifically for derrickhands).WHMIS and TDG (Transportation of Dangerous Goods).
WHMIS and TDG (Transportation of Dangerous Goods).
Licensing: A driver's license appropriate to the class of vehicle (Class 1 or 3) is required for well service operators.
salary_prospects: Note: Annual earnings often reach $85,000 - $110,000 when including overtime and remote site premiums common in the energy sector.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady increase in "Well Completion" activities as Canada optimizes its existing reservoirs. The retirement of the "Boomer" generation from the oil patch is creating an urgent need for younger, mid-level operators, making 2026 an ideal time for international workers to enter the field.
labour_market_demand: The demand for drilling and service operators is Very Good, particularly in the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. In 2026, Alberta, Saskatchewan, and British Columbia are reporting significant "Hiring Difficulty" indices due to an aging workforce and the expansion of natural gas projects. Energy companies are actively utilizing LMIA-backed recruitment to bring in international operators who can handle the increasing technical complexity of modern well sites.
Technical Advancement: Gain hands-on experience with automated pipe-handling systems and specialized chemical "fracking" units.
High Entry-Level Pay: Even at the TEER 4 level, this sector offers some of the highest starting wages in Canada compared to other service industries.
Career Progression: A clear and rapid pathway exists to move into TEER 3 Driller (NOC 83101) or TEER 2 Supervisor (NOC 82021) roles with experience.
Economic Stability: Benefit from Canada’s long-term energy infrastructure projects and the rising global demand for Canadian natural gas.
pathways: New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A high-priority stream for workers already in Canada contributing to the essential energy sector.
Alberta Opportunity Stream: A fast-track for operators currently working in Alberta with a valid job offer.
Saskatchewan INP (Semi-Skilled Agriculture/Natural Resources): Accessible for workers with at least 6 months of experience in the province.
Rural Community Immigration Pilot (RCIP): Targeting workers for energy-dependent rural communities in Western Canada.
